Some researchers believe that life may have originated in small water pools with changing environmental conditions rather than in the oceans. Hot springs are examples of such environments. In India, the hot... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 2.1 How to Study Cells?, 10 Exploration|Grade 9, 2.2 Structure of a Cell, 2.2.1 Cell membrane — The universal feature of a cell. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Is Matter Around Us Pure is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that it is widely accepted amongst the scientific community that life originated in water.

The text explains that some researchers believe that life may have originated in small water pools with changing environmental conditions rather than in the oceans.

The text explains that hot springs are examples of such environments.

The text explains that in India, the hot springs of Puga Valley in Ladakh maintain very high temperatures (nearly at the boiling point of water) even in a cold climate.

The text explains that these environmental conditions seem to be similar to those on the early Earth, about 3.5 billion years ago.

The text explains that the organisms living in these hot springs are mostly heat-loving bacteria called thermophiles, which are unicellular.

The text explains that scientists from the Birbal Sahni Institute of Palaeosciences, Lucknow, studied these hot springs and found that calcium carbonate formed rapidly around them.

The text explains that these deposits may have protected early organic molecules from harmful radiation and extreme conditions, and they may have also helped in the formation of the first protective membrane — the barrier that defines a cell.

The text explains that the cell represents the basic level at which life exists.

The text explains that some organisms, such as bacteria or yeast consist of only one cell (unicellular), while others like plants, fish, birds or humans are made up of millions of cells (multicellular) that work together.

The text explains that a group of similar cells performing similar Cell: The Building Block of Life Chapter 2 y Where does a cell come from? y How have technological interventions facilitated the creation of new knowledge in understanding the world beyond the naked eye? y How is the cell structural and functional unit of life? y How does a cell multiply?

The text explains that cell: The Building Block of Life 9 functions forms tissues.

The text explains that different tissues are organised to form an organ and several organs work together to form organ systems.

The text explains that such as, nasal pores, nasal cavity, trachea and lungs form respiratory system.

The text explains that even when cells are organised into tissues, organs, and organ systems the cell remains the fundamental unit of structure and function in all living organisms.

The text explains that this makes us wonder how such tiny cells perform so many different activities.

The text explains that in this chapter, we will explore the answers to these questions as we enter the fascinating world of cells!

The text explains that this raises an important question — how have cell biologists studied the structure and function of cells that are much smaller than the limit of resolution of the human eye?

The text explains that in school laboratories, light microscopes are used to observe objects using Grade 8 Curiosity Chapter 10 1 km 100 m Human height Rocket Chicken egg Fish egg Most plant and animal cells Nucleus Most bacteria Mitochondrion Smallest bacteria Ribosomes Viruses Proteins Lipids Small molecules Atoms 10 m 1 m 0.1 m 1 cm 1 mm 100 μm 10 μm 1 μm 10 nm 100 nm 1 nm 0.1 nm Unaided eye Light microscope Electron microscope C Amoeba Neem Tree Great Indian Bustard Fig.

The text explains that 2.1: Size of the objects and its visibility through unaided to aided eye Grade 8 Curiosity Chapter 2

The text explains that 2.2) in your school laboratory and use it to observe fine structures of various materials.

The text explains that 2.2: Structure of a light microscope Eye piece Body tube Objective lens Stage Mirror Course adjustment knob Fine adjustment knob Handle Base Suppose, 25 cells are seen along the diameter.

The text explains that thus, a microscope allows us to see very small structures clearly and is an essential tool for studying the cell structure.

The text explains that over the years, scientists have improved the microscope by improving its three main features — resolution (measure of clarity), contrast (the difference in brightness between various Ready to Go Beyond Fig.

The text explains that 2.3: An electron microscope Apart from light microscopes, scientists also use powerful electron microscopes that reveal the fine details of a cell structure.

The text explains that these instruments use a beam of electrons instead of light to produce highly magnified images, allowing us to see cell structure at the nanometre scale with remarkable clarity (a nanometre is one-billionth of a metre).

The text explains that 2.2 Structure of a Cell You have learnt that cells are organised into specialised tissues and organs, and collectively perform specific function.

The text explains that 2.2.1 Cell membrane — The universal feature of a cell The cell membrane is a thin boundary that surrounds a cell and protects its contents.

The text explains that how does the structure of the cell membrane in the cells of alveoli control the movement of substances across it?

The text explains that in plants, water from the soil enters root cells by this process of osmosis. a cell is kept in salt or sugar solutions of different concentrations?

The text explains that the fluid-mosaic model explains its structure (Fig.

The text explains that 2.7: Structure of a cell membrane Lipid bilayer Various types of proteins mung bean seeds are kept in a concentrated solution after soaking in water for 12 hours?

The text explains that 2.2.2 Cell wall — The outer covering of cells In general, plants cannot move from place to place, so they need a rigid structure to withstand environmental stresses like wind and rain.

The text explains that the cell wall also helps leaves and flowers remain firm, and maintain their shapes and help plants stay upright.

The text explains that although rigid the cell wall is permeable, which means water and some dissolved minerals can pass through it.

The text explains that pause and Ponder 2.3 The Cell Interior — A Coordinated Working System You have learnt that most cells have three basic parts: y a selectively permeable membrane called the plasma membrane, y a semi-fluid, jelly-like substance called the cytoplasm, and y a prominent nucleus.

The text explains that observe the different structures present in each of them.

The text explains that such cells are called prokaryotic cells (pro means primitive and karyon means Grade 7 Curiosity Chapter 9 (Genetic material)

The text explains that in prokaryotic cells, most cellular activities take place directly in the cytoplasm.

The text explains that cell structures Bacterial cell Plant cell Animal cell 1.

The text explains that eukaryotic cells carry out various life processes in different cell organelles independently at the same time.

The text explains that let us explore these specialised structures present inside a cell.

The text explains that the nucleus contains chromosomes, which are visible as rod-shaped structures only when the cell is about to divide.

The text explains that chromatin material is visible as an entangled mass of thread-like structures.

The text explains that 2.11: Structure of a nucleus Nuclear pore Double-layered nuclear membrane Chromatin Nucleolus Nucleus

The text explains that cell: The Building Block of Life 17 Ribosomes — The protein factories These are tiny structures may be present either freely in the cytoplasm or attached to the endoplasmic reticulum.

The text explains that recognising that a significant amount of produce may be lost post-harvest, she employed a traditional yet scientifically sound method to extend the shelf life of amla and lemons.

The text explains that she turned perishable produce into profitable products, such as pickles and sharbat.

The text explains that she used the excess produce to prepare pickles, murabbas, and sharbat by adding appropriate amounts of salt, sugar, or jaggery to small pieces of fruit and their juices.

The text explains that these were then stored in small glass bottles for sale, helping her prevent the wastage of post-harvest produce.

The text explains that this shift from farming to agro-processing would strengthen food security and boost the local economy, creating a sustainable model that cuts waste while increasing her income.

The text explains that (ii) How does the addition of high concentrations of salt and sugar create an environment that prevents the growth of spoilage-causing bacteria and fungi?

Source: NCERT Class 9 Science, Chapter 2: Is Matter Around Us Pure
